responses.strategic_rationale: This 3-year residential parasomnia research unit navigates three fundamental tensions: (1) ecological validity vs. operational complexity—the facility must capture naturalistic behavior while managing safety and logistics for infrequent events; (2) data richness vs. participant burden—multi-modal sensing and extended stays must yield actionable phenotypes without excessive participant fatigue or attrition; (3) scientific rigor vs. pragmatic feasibility—ambitious event-capture targets and dual-scored annotations demand sustainable staffing and recruitment pipelines. The selected levers address these core conflicts by questioning participant selection thresholds, monitoring intensity, facility footprint scaling, event-triage automation scope, control-group design, data governance, and funding contingency—each carrying explicit trade-offs between safety/burden, cost/statistical power, and speed/rigor.

levers.name: Participant Inclusion Threshold and Length-of-Stay Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Lowering the self-reported episode frequency threshold from ≥2 per month to ≥1 per month increases recruiting pool by approximately 40–50%, accelerating enrollment. → Systemic: Lower-frequency participants will occupy beds longer (potentially 8–10 weeks vs. 4–6 weeks) to achieve event capture targets, reducing annual throughput from ~35 to ~25 participants and increasing per-participant facility cost by 25–30%, but improving statistical power for rare phenotypes and temporal clustering analysis. → Strategic: This decision trades higher recruitment velocity and inclusion breadth against extended occupancy, staff coordination burden, and risk of participant drop-out during prolonged stays; it directly impacts the 50–70 enrollment target and event-capture ceiling at month 18.
options: Retain the ≥2 episodes/month criterion, accepting lower recruitment volume but shorter median stays (4–6 weeks), faster throughput, and earlier completion of the 50–70 target with reduced operational friction.

Lower the threshold to ≥1 episode/month contingent on bed-partner collateral confirmation and prior polysomnography evidence, moderately expanding the pool while maintaining event-likelihood confidence.

Adopt a two-tier admission pathway: fast-track participants with ≥3 episodes/month for 2–4 week focused stays, and slow-track participants with ≥1 episode/month enrolled on a staggered rolling basis with planned 8–10 week residential sequences to maximize event yield and phenotypic depth.
levers.review_lever: Controls event-yield confidence vs. facility utilization and participant retention risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the financial and ethical implications of longer stays on participant well-being, caregiver burden, and the facility's competitive positioning for recruitment in a limited regional market.

levers.name: Sensing Architecture and Nightly Data Burden
levers.consequences: Immediate: The tiered model (routine dry-electrode EEG headbands + contact-free sensors; enhanced PSG on scheduled nights; escalation on technician flagging) balances naturalistic sleep preservation against clinical-grade capture. → Systemic: Full-montage PSG only ~20% of nights reduces equipment wear and technician setup time by ~70%, lowering per-night operational cost by €150–200, but scheduled nights may miss rare event clustering that occurs off-schedule; technician event-flagging introduces subjective triage variability with inter-rater agreement typically 0.65–0.75 for borderline arousals. → Strategic: This hybrid approach trades comprehensive overnight physiological resolution (which would suppress sleep quality and inflate equipment/labor costs) against the risk of missing low-frequency recurrences and introducing annotation bias; it directly impacts data quality sufficiency for Aim 3 algorithm development.
options: Implement full PSG every night across all 8 active suites, ensuring maximal capture sensitivity and standardized scoring inputs, accepting 30–40% increased nightly equipment cost (€1,200–1,600), accelerated technician fatigue, and potential sleep suppression in participants.

Maintain the three-tiered model with scheduled enhanced nights on days 1, mid-stay, and final night plus technician-flagged escalation, balancing clinical rigor with ecological validity and accepting documented inter-rater disagreement on ambiguous arousals as an inherent research trade-off.

Deploy continuous wearable EEG + actigraphy during routine nights with machine-learning event candidate flagging (replacing technician judgment), reducing subjective variability but requiring algorithm pre-training on historical data and introducing dependency on third-party software validation before deployment.
levers.review_lever: Controls sleep naturalism and participant burden vs. diagnostic comprehensiveness and data standardization. Weakness: The options fail to address the resource cost of training technicians to consistent flagging standards and the ethical implications of exposing participants to different sensing intensities based on probabilistic event models rather than uniform protocols.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Sequencing and Bed Capacity Management
levers.consequences: Immediate: Launching with 8 active suites (vs. physical capacity of 12) reduces opening-month overhead and staffing complexity while preserving future expansion optionality. → Systemic: Year-one operations at 8 suites allow evaluation of safe staffing ratios, false-alarm management, and annotation throughput before committing to 12 suites; if year-one data show 3-person night shift can safely cover 10+ suites with <5% response-time delays, expansion to 12 suites in year two increases annual participant throughput by 50% and spreads fixed overhead, reducing per-bed annual cost from €475K to €320K. → Strategic: This phased approach trades initial underutilization (estimated 60–70% bed occupancy in months 1–12) and higher year-one per-participant cost against risk mitigation; rapid expansion without operational validation could trigger safety incidents, data-quality degradation, or staff burnout that derails the entire 50–70 target.
options: Fully renovate and activate all 12 suites at launch, maximizing year-one participant throughput and achieving the 50–70 enrollment target by month 24, accepting staffing scalability risk and higher operational overhead if data quality or safety proves suboptimal.

Maintain the planned 8-suite pilot launch, defer expansion to month 18 conditional on demonstrated safe staffing ratios and event-capture yields, and extend year-three operations if month 18 review confirms readiness, trading slower enrollment ramp for lower risk.

Open 8 suites initially with infrastructure pre-positioned for rapid modular expansion to 10 suites by month 9 (staffing and equipment already budgeted), enabling mid-pilot acceleration if early cohorts show high event-yield rates and low safety incident frequency.
levers.review_lever: Controls financial leverage and operational risk vs. enrollment velocity and year-one cost-per-participant. Weakness: The options fail to explicitly address the fixed costs of property lease, renovation, and core staff (PI, coordinator, data engineer) that remain constant regardless of occupancy, potentially rendering the 8-suite launch financially unsustainable if participant recruitment falls below 15–20 per year.

levers.name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Algorithm Scope and Validation Standard
levers.consequences: Immediate: Developing a machine-learning triage tool to rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden advances Aim 3 and shortens publication timelines by 3–6 months. → Systemic: If the algorithm achieves sensitivity ≥0.80 and specificity ≥0.75 against dual human scoring, technician workload for borderline event classification decreases by ~40%, freeing ~15 hours/week for extended annotation of confirmed events and quality assurance; however, algorithm reliance introduces dependency on model retraining if participant demographics or sensor configurations shift, and false negatives (missed events) carry clinical liability even with prominent disclaimers. → Strategic: This initiative trades annotation burden reduction and operational efficiency against the risk of algorithmic bias (the model may systematically underdetect rare phenotypes in underrepresented participants) and the ethical tension between research reproducibility (requiring human adjudication of all borderline cases) and operational pragmatism (using the algorithm to accelerate triage).
options: Develop the event-triage algorithm as a research contribution only, trained on year-one and year-two dual-scored data, published with sensitivity/specificity metrics and cross-validation, but explicitly prohibited from operationally filtering technician annotation streams during data collection.

Train and deploy the algorithm to rank candidate events during real-time monitoring, with human scorers reviewing all algorithm-flagged candidates, reducing initial review burden by ~30–40% and enabling technicians to focus on annotation depth rather than exhaustive screening.

Implement a two-model cascade: a conservative sensitivity-maximizing model (≥0.90 sensitivity, ~0.60 specificity) for initial candidate detection, followed by a specificity-optimizing model for confirmation, balancing event capture with false-positive reduction and enabling technician prioritization of high-confidence events for detailed annotation.
levers.review_lever: Controls operational efficiency vs. research rigor and algorithmic transparency. Weakness: The options fail to establish a pre-planned mechanism for detecting and correcting algorithmic drift over time, such as quarterly blind validation against a held-out human-scored subset, and do not address how the algorithm's performance will be communicated to referring clinicians or participants if future clinical deployment is proposed.

levers.name: Control Group Matching Strategy and Baseline Scope
levers.consequences: Immediate: Enrolling matched controls (age, sex, sleep duration) in identical residential monitoring captures normative nocturnal movement, arousal, and false-positive sensor activity to benchmark the event-triage algorithm. → Systemic: Control recruitment adds ~€120K annually (~20 participants × €6K per-participant all-in cost), increasing year-one budget by ~7%, but enables benchmarking of sensor false-positive rates and arousal thresholds; without controls, the algorithm's specificity claims rest on post-hoc comparisons to published normative data, introducing literature-dependency bias and publication-lag artifacts (normative databases may be outdated or derived from different populations). → Strategic: This decision trades upfront budget cost and recruitment complexity against methodological rigor and the credibility of negative findings (the claim that rare arousals are truly parasomnia-specific rather than variants of normal sleep physiology); it directly impacts the strength of Aim 3 algorithm benchmarking and the generalizability of phenotyping results.
options: Recruit and enroll 15–20 matched healthy controls in the identical residential protocol, matching on age, sex, and self-reported sleep duration, to generate internal baseline data and directly benchmark sensor false-positive rates and arousal distributions.

Rely on published normative polysomnography databases (e.g., SHHS, MESA) and simulator-based sensor false-positive characterization to define baseline arousal and movement thresholds, deferring formal control enrollment and reducing budget and recruitment burden by ~€120K over three years.

Implement a hybrid approach: enroll 8–10 controls in year one for intensive sensor false-positive characterization and validation of arousal detection thresholds, then transition to literature-based normative comparisons in years two and three, balancing early methodological validation with later budget reallocation toward case enrollment.
levers.review_lever: Controls internal validity and sensor-specific baseline characterization vs. budget efficiency and recruitment feasibility. Weakness: The options fail to address the ethical and practical challenge of recruiting healthy controls willing to undergo 2–8 week residential stays for minimal scientific benefit to themselves, and do not specify how sex/age stratification will be implemented if the parasomnia case cohort is imbalanced (e.g., predominantly female confusional arousals vs. male sleep terrors).

levers.name: Data Sharing Governance and Repository Selection
levers.consequences: Immediate: Committing to deposit de-identified physiological and sensor data (excluding video) in a recognized repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, XNAT) enables rapid data sharing and supports reproducible Aim 3 algorithm benchmarking by external teams. → Systemic: Public data release requires 6–12 months post-collection for de-identification review, consent-form amendment, and institutional repository approval, delaying external validation by ~1 year; however, public release attracts secondary analysis from computational neuroscience groups, multiplying the scientific impact per participant and potentially accelerating algorithm development from current timelines (single internal team) to distributed multi-team iteration, estimated 30–50% acceleration. → Strategic: This initiative trades publication-control and participant privacy concerns (even de-identified EEG + video metadata may enable re-identification in small networks) against scientific velocity and openness; it also signals credibility to funding agencies and referral clinicians that the team operates under FAIR principles rather than data hoarding, improving recruitment narratives.
options: Restrict data release to collaborating institutional teams under formal data use agreements, retaining full publication priority, minimizing privacy risk, and allowing the core team to develop the event-triage algorithm without external competition.

Deposit all de-identified physiological, EEG, and sensor data (bedside video explicitly excluded) in OpenNeuro or similar BIDS-compliant public repository within 18 months of collection, enabling external algorithm development and secondary phenotypic analyses, accepting 12-month publication-timeline delays for the core team's methods papers.

Implement a tiered release strategy: physiological and EEG summary statistics published openly at month 12; raw de-identified EEG and sensor streams released to the public repository at month 24; video and metadata retained under institutional access only with explicit external-collaborator agreements requiring co-authorship on derivative publications.
levers.review_lever: Controls publication priority and team competitive advantage vs. scientific openness and algorithm development velocity. Weakness: The options fail to specify the legal and technical infrastructure required for managing participant withdrawal requests post-publication (if a control participant requests data removal after public deposit, rectification or suppression mechanisms are limited and may compromise reproducibility), and do not address liability if de-identified data are later re-identified through cross-linking with other public health datasets.

levers.name: Pharma Partnership Integration and Conditional Funding Contingency
levers.consequences: Immediate: Securing a pharmaceutical industry partnership provides optional upside funding (estimated €300K–500K additional for algorithm deployment or expanded cohort phases) without compromising academic independence (partner receives 30-day pre-publication comment window only). → Systemic: If secured by month 6, pharma funding could accelerate year-two operations by ~20%, enabling enrollment of 40–45 participants in year two (vs. planned 30–35) and supporting expanded neuroimaging or biomarker substudies, increasing sample power for future intervention trials; if not secured by month 12, the facility remains viable on DFG + University funding alone, though contingent year-three expansion (€800K) becomes dependent on documented month-24 progress, introducing conditional risk. → Strategic: This lever balances financial resilience (pharma upside reduces dependency on single-source DFG funding) against mission creep (partnerships may introduce implicit expectations for clinically deployable tools, conflicting with the stated early-stage research mission) and stakeholder complexity (managing pharma visibility and intellectual property boundaries alongside academic publication timelines).
options: Actively pursue pharmaceutical partnerships starting month 2, targeting sleep-disorder medication companies with commercial interests in nocturnal seizure discrimination or parasomnia phenomenology, negotiating binding partnership agreements by month 6 with explicit publication-rights retention and 30-day pre-publication review, accepting the operational burden of partner liaison and reporting.

Maintain openness to opportunistic partnerships if companies approach unsolicited, but do not allocate recruitment or business-development resources toward securing partnerships, allowing year-one and year-two operations to proceed entirely on DFG and University funding, and treating conditional year-three funding as internally sustainable if month-24 milestones are met.

Establish a formal pharma advisory panel (non-binding, quarterly meetings) comprising representatives from 2–3 sleep-disorder medication companies, providing early access to methods validation and preliminary phenotyping results in exchange for in-kind sensor equipment or data-analysis software licenses (reducing budget pressure), while retaining full publication autonomy and deferring formal financial partnership until evidence of algorithm utility emerges.
levers.review_lever: Controls financial flexibility and contingency resilience vs. mission focus and regulatory complexity. Weakness: The options fail to address the governance ambiguity if a pharma partner claims intellectual property rights over algorithm improvements or phenotypic discoveries with potential commercial applications, and do not specify how the external scientific advisory board will oversee conflicts of interest if a board member has financial ties to a partnership company.

responses.summary: These seven levers are well-balanced across facility operations (expansion sequencing, sensing architecture), recruitment and retention (participant inclusion thresholds, control strategy), algorithm development (event-triage scope and validation), and governance (data sharing, pharma partnerships). Each lever includes explicit trade-offs between core tensions (safety vs. throughput, rigor vs. efficiency, independence vs. financial resilience). However, a critical missing dimension is the **staffing and technician training sustainability**—the plan assumes three technicians can safely manage 8 suites with event-response and annotation capacity, but the levers do not probe the realistic on-call burden, shift-rotation fatigue, and annotation-scoring consistency (inter-rater reliability among rotating technicians). Add to **Lever 2 (Sensing Architecture)** the option: 'Establish a formalized technician training and competency-certification protocol, with monthly blind inter-rater reliability audits (target >0.80 Fleiss' kappa across all three technicians) and a threshold rule (if two consecutive audits fall below 0.75, pause new admissions and retrain), accepting 2–4 week enrollment delays but ensuring annotation consistency and reducing legal liability from de facto diagnostic variability.' This operationalizes the implicit assumption that rotating staff will maintain consistent judgment despite fatigue and cognitive load over 36 months.

responses.strategic_rationale: This research unit navigates a fundamental tension between methodological rigor and operational sustainability. The project must capture rare parasomnia events in naturalistic conditions (requiring extended residential stays and continuous sensing) while managing staff fatigue, false-alarm burden, and cost efficiency. The seven levers below address the core decision points: how to balance capture yield against admission length; how to evolve the sensor architecture as confidence grows; how to staff safely without burnout; how to validate event detection without over-automating clinical judgment; how to manage participant safety risk during live events; how to fund sustainably without compromising academic independence; and how to scale evidence-gathering for future interventions. These levers are critical because they force the team to choose between competing operational, scientific, and financial constraints that will define whether the facility achieves its aims or collapses under logistical or financial pressure.

levers.name: Nocturnal Event Capture Strategy and Admission Length Ceiling
levers.consequences: Immediate: Shorter target stays (e.g., 2–4 weeks maximum) reduce per-participant cost and accelerate enrollment velocity, but longer stays (6–10 weeks) capture more rare events and strengthen within-person longitudinal phenotyping. Systemic: A 4-week ceiling admits 50–60 participants annually at current capacity (vs. 30–35 with 8-week average), increasing event yield by ~40% and publication readiness but risking 25–30% of participants departing with zero captured events, triggering protocol reviews. Strategic: This trade-off determines whether the facility achieves Aim 2's longitudinal characterization depth or instead produces a broader cross-sectional snapshot with weaker individual trajectories.
options: Implement a strict 3-week baseline stay with optional 2-week extension only if ≥1 event is captured, capping total length at 5 weeks and prioritizing enrollment velocity over individual event density.

Adopt a flexible 4–8 week admission protocol calibrated by participant symptom history and first-week event yield, with extension contingent on technician assessment and PI adjudication, balancing yield and retention.

Pursue a 6-week target stay for all participants regardless of event capture, with an optional enrichment phase for zero-event cases using enhanced sensor protocols or provocation strategies (e.g., sleep deprivation or alcohol), maximizing phenotypic depth but increasing cost and safety complexity.
levers.review_lever: Controls capture rate vs. operational efficiency: shorter stays accelerate enrollment but leave many participants without adjudicated events, while longer stays deepen phenotyping but slow throughput and increase staff and facility burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant fatigue and withdrawal risk, which may be high in clinical populations and could undermine data quality or safety compliance in prolonged stays.

levers.name: Night Technician Annotation Authority and Real-Time Scoring Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Granting night technicians real-time annotation authority (flagging probable events during observation) accelerates event detection and reduces next-day backlogs but introduces subjective bias and potential false positives that downstream dual-rater scoring must resolve. Systemic: A technician-empowered model reduces per-event review burden by ~30–50% (technician-flagged events require only confirming dual review vs. full-night review) but increases first-pass false-positive rate to ~15–20%, creating rework and potential signal loss if annotations are too permissive. Strategic: This lever directly affects Aim 3's event-triage pipeline—if technician flags are reliable, they become ground-truth training labels; if noisy, they pollute algorithm benchmarking and delay publication.
options: Restrict night technicians to passive real-time observation with written descriptions but no formal event claims, requiring all event adjudication to occur in post-hoc dual-rater review, ensuring consistency but creating next-morning annotation backlogs and limiting late-night clinical response.

Empower technicians to flag suspected events in real time with a three-tier confidence system (high, moderate, low), using high-confidence flags to trigger immediate clinical evaluation and as anchors for post-hoc dual review, with low-confidence flags subjected to full-night re-review.

Establish a technician-radiologist model where technicians flag events in real time and immediately call a sleeping on-site clinician (on a rotation) to perform live assessment and provisional adjudication, creating the richest phenotypic capture but requiring expensive 24/7 physician presence and risking clinician sleep deprivation.
levers.review_lever: Controls real-time clinical response vs. methodological purity: technician authority enables live safety intervention and faster downstream analysis but introduces subjective bias, while strict post-hoc review preserves consistency but delays response and increases annotation burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider training variability—different technicians may have very different annotation thresholds, and no option specifies how to validate inter-technician agreement or standardize flagging criteria at the outset.

levers.name: Cross-Night PSG Montage Distribution and Enhanced-Monitoring Intensity
levers.consequences: Immediate: Concentrating full PSG montage on first, mid-stay, and final nights (current plan) preserves cost and reduces equipment fatigue (~3 intensive nights per 4-week stay) but may miss episode morphology variation if events cluster unpredictably. Systemic: Increasing enhanced-night frequency to every 3rd night raises PSG equipment utilization by ~50%, improving morphological diversity capture and algorithm training data by approximately 25–35% but increasing technician annotation load by 40% and equipment maintenance costs by 30%. Strategic: This choice determines the richness of Aim 2 phenotypic characterization and the statistical power of Aim 3 algorithm benchmarking; sparse enhanced nights may force reliance on dry-sensor data to infer morphology, weakening claims about sleep-stage specificity.
options: Maintain the current schedule of three fixed enhanced nights (first, mid, stay, final) per participant, ensuring consistency and cost predictability while accepting morphological blind spots between scheduled days.

Deploy a reactive enhanced-night protocol triggered by technician-flagged events, adding full PSG within 48 hours of any suspected parasomnia capture, ensuring morphological detail around real events but creating variable staffing demand and potential montage-sequencing artifacts.

Implement a high-frequency enhanced protocol with full PSG montage every third night for all participants, generating denser morphological coverage and richer algorithm training data at the cost of increased equipment wear, technician burden, and per-participant cost increase of ~€800–1200.
levers.review_lever: Controls data richness vs. operational burden: sparse enhanced-night scheduling keeps costs and logistics manageable but risks phenotypic blindness between monitoring days, while dense scheduling maximizes capture detail but strains technician annotation capacity and equipment. Weakness: The options fail to account for participant tolerance of repeated electrode application/removal cycles; frequent PSG nights may increase skin irritation, sleep disruption, and dropout risk in an already vulnerable population.

levers.name: Sensor Ecosystem Maturation and Technology Confidence Thresholds
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deploying the planned tiered model (dry EEG, mattress sensors, infrared) from day one distributes risk but creates complex data fusion challenges and high annotation burden if sensor false-positive rates are unknown. Systemic: A phased confidence-building approach (piloting dry EEG for 8 weeks, then adding mattress/infrared once reliability is documented) delays full-suite deployment by 2–3 months but reduces annotation rework by ~40% and builds technician competence; conversely, immediate parallel deployment achieves faster methodological maturity but may produce 2–4 months of unusable or contradictory sensor data that requires re-collection. Strategic: This timing decision affects publication timeline, participant enrollment schedule, and the credibility of Aim 1 (operational validation) because false-positive or unreliable sensor streams could force facility closure or protocol amendment if not identified and managed early.
options: Launch with the full planned sensor stack (dry EEG, mattress, infrared, microphone, environment) simultaneously across all suites, accepting initial integration noise and relying on post-hoc data quality review and technician feedback to identify and suppress unreliable streams after 8–12 weeks of operation.

Implement a phased sensor rollout beginning with dry-electrode EEG and infrared only (simplest validation) for the first cohort (weeks 1–12), then adding mattress sensors once dry-EEG false-positive and sensitivity metrics are documented, scaling to full stack by month 4.

Establish a small dedicated sensor-validation cohort of 5–8 healthy control participants who undergo 1-week intensive stays with all planned sensors plus intensive technician and video oversight, conducting offline sensor agreement studies before opening to clinical participants, delaying full enrollment but guaranteeing data quality from day one.
levers.review_lever: Controls deployment speed vs. data quality assurance: simultaneous full-stack deployment accelerates methodological maturity but creates validation confusion early on, while phased rollout builds confidence incrementally but delays the generation of Aim 3 training data. Weakness: The options fail to specify success criteria for sensor validation (e.g., what sensitivity or specificity threshold triggers progression to the next phase), leaving the decision to advance ad hoc rather than criteria-driven.

levers.name: Live Event Response Staffing Model and Injury-Prevention Activation
levers.consequences: Immediate: A minimal-response model (technician observer + remote on-call physician) is cost-efficient (~€120K annually for on-call coverage) but cannot guarantee immediate physical intervention if a participant risks falling or self-injury. Systemic: A full-time overnight clinician (nurse or EMT) present in the facility increases labor costs by ~€140K annually and ensures <2-minute response time, reducing injury incidents by an estimated 70–80% (based on comparable sleep-lab data) but creates occupancy stress and potential over-intervention if the clinician lacks parasomnia expertise. Strategic: This lever shapes the safety profile reported in Aim 1 validation and institutional review; if injury incidents occur and are attributed to understaffing, the facility may lose ethics approval or face liability, while over-staffing increases cost and may trigger a year-three budget overrun.
options: Employ a single night technician per 4 suites with a remotely available on-call sleep physician (contacted via phone within 5 minutes) and written protocols for technician-initiated first aid and bed-side padded barriers, accepting injury risk in line with residential sleep centers and relying on pre-admission risk screening to exclude highest-danger participants.

Staff each night shift with one full-time overnight nurse or EMT co-located in the facility alongside the technician, trained in parasomnia first aid and authorized to perform immediate non-pharmacologic intervention (guiding, reassurance, environmental modification), increasing response speed to <1 minute and injury prevention at known cost of €140K annually.

Implement a two-clinician model for the first 12 weeks (pilot validation phase) with concurrent physician and paramedic presence, then transition to single-nurse staffing after demonstrating safety metrics, providing maximum early confidence in operational safety while managing long-term cost to ~€100K annually post-pilot.
levers.review_lever: Controls injury prevention vs. cost efficiency: full-time clinical presence minimizes injury risk but is expensive and may create clinical over-reach, while minimal staffing reduces cost but places burden on pre-screening and participant compliance with safety protocols. Weakness: The options fail to address what happens during escalation events (e.g., a participant having a prolonged confusional arousal with agitation); none specifies authority for sedation, physical restraint, or emergency-service callout, leaving de facto protocol gaps.

levers.name: Pharmaceutical Partnership Governance and Commercial Data Access Boundaries
levers.consequences: Immediate: A formal pharma partnership (e.g., a company testing a novel parasomnia therapeutic) provides non-dilutive funding (~€300–500K over 3 years) and expanded analysis scope but requires pharma access to de-identified physiological datasets and early data on treatment-relevant biomarkers. Systemic: If pharma access is granted, the academic team retains publication independence (per current plan) but pharma's 30-day pre-publication review window may delay publication by 4–6 months on average (based on precedent) and introduces reputational risk if pharma is perceived as suppressing negative findings; conversely, rejecting pharma funding preserves publication autonomy but forces the project to absorb an estimated 30–40% higher per-participant cost from federal and institutional sources. Strategic: This decision affects both the financial runway (month 24 contingency gate) and Aim 1 credibility; if pharma-funded and a safety incident occurs, independent oversight may question whether economic incentives shaped risk tolerance.
options: Decline formal pharma partnerships entirely, relying solely on DFG and University of Bonn funding, ensuring complete publication independence and participant-privacy autonomy but potentially forcing cost cuts or slower enrollment if funding is delayed.

Establish a limited-scope pharma collaboration under a material-transfer and data-use agreement restricting pharma access to de-identified physiology only (excluding video, participant identifiers, and sensor metadata), with 30-day pre-publication review but no editorial authority, and pharma co-authorship only if scientific contribution merits it.

Pursue a full pharma partnership model with shared governance, pharma co-funding of Aim 3 algorithm work, pharma access to de-identified physiological and clinical outcomes data under signed data-use agreement, and pharma co-authorship on algorithm benchmarking papers, funding 40% of year-two and year-three costs in exchange for early-access pre-publication data.
levers.review_lever: Controls academic independence vs. financial sustainability: pharma partnership accelerates funding and broadens analysis scope but introduces commercial influence and publication delays, while independence preserves autonomy but increases reliance on government funding and may force operational constraints. Weakness: The options fail to address what happens if pharma's compound is found to be ineffective or harmful based on the facility's data; none specifies whether pharma retains any editorial influence over that reporting or whether the academic team retains unambiguous authority to publish negative results without delay.

levers.name: Recruitment Inclusivity Scope and Diagnostic Flexibility at Screening
levers.consequences: Immediate: A strict enrollment criterion requiring AASM-confirmed NREM parasomnia diagnosis (via specialist interview and screening PSG) ensures diagnostic homogeneity but excludes borderline cases (suspected parasomnia mimics, atypical presentations, or patients with prior misdiagnosis) and slows recruitment. Systemic: Expanding inclusion to 'suspected' parasomnia cases without mandatory pre-admission PSG accelerates enrollment by ~35–50% and captures phenotypic diversity (including undiagnosed REM-behavior-disorder mimics or sleep-related hypoventilation misattributed to parasomnia) but increases protocol deviations and safety risk, as some admitted participants may have contraindications (e.g., undetected epilepsy); dual-rater event adjudication provides safety net, increasing scoring burden by ~20–30%. Strategic: This choice directly affects Aim 1 (facility operationalization) and Aim 2 (phenotypic characterization); strict inclusion yields clean science but slow enrollment, while flexible inclusion achieves targets faster but risks publication critique for diagnostic heterogeneity and potential safety incidents.
options: Maintain the current strict enrollment criterion requiring documented NREM parasomnia history via clinical specialist interview and prior or screening-protocol polysomnography, with PI adjudication of all edge cases, ensuring diagnostic consistency but accepting slower recruitment pace and excluding phenotypically interesting borderline cases.

Adopt a clinical-interview-only pathway (no mandatory pre-admission PSG) for recruits with clear collateral history (bed-partner corroboration of typical parasomnia semiology), with PI clinical judgment as the gate and formal diagnostic confirmation deferred to post-admission full-PSG review, accelerating enrollment while accepting higher protocol-deviation risk and downstream adjudication complexity.

Implement an expanded-phenotype protocol accepting both confirmed NREM parasomnias and 'suspected parasomnia or parasomnia-like behavior' with documented sleep disruption and family history, requiring all admissions to complete full diagnostic workup (PSG, video-EEG, neurologic screening per RBD protocol) on night one, broadening the scientific scope and recruitment base but requiring enhanced week-one clinical capacity and risk-screening protocols.
levers.review_lever: Controls diagnostic purity vs. recruitment efficiency: strict pre-admission confirmation ensures methodological rigor but limits recruitment speed and phenotypic diversity, while flexible intake accelerates enrollment and captures edge cases but increases safety and validity risk. Weakness: The options fail to specify how the facility will manage participants admitted under flexible criteria who are later found to have contraindications (e.g., undetected epilepsy on diagnostic PSG); none addresses whether they remain in the study, are withdrawn, or transition to a separate protocol, leaving a potential ethical and scientific governance gap.

responses.summary: The seven levers are well-balanced across operational, scientific, and financial domains and challenge core assumptions about capture yield, staff scope, technical maturity, and participant inclusion. However, a critical missing dimension is **participant engagement and retention management**—the plan assumes participants will tolerate weeks of monitoring, multiple electrode applications, and close technician observation, but provides no strategy for managing isolation, sleep disruption from monitoring itself, or dropout risk in a potentially vulnerable population. **Add a new lever: 'Participant Support and Retention Strategy' to address whether the facility deploys a dedicated psychologist or peer-support model, on-site daytime activities, or remote family communication systems to improve compliance and data-quality completion, versus a minimal-contact model that maximizes naturalistic conditions but risks high attrition.** Additionally, the levers would benefit from explicit cross-lever sequencing: for example, Lever 1 (admission length) and Lever 4 (sensor maturity) are interdependent—short stays with unvalidated sensors may yield no adjudicated events, while long stays with a phased sensor rollout may miss phenotypic detail.
